justification: All pass criteria met: Option B recommended with 5+ explicit reasons (retry via queue semantics, channel extensibility via new consumers, decoupling from provider failures, DLQ for reliability, latency budget unlocks async). Trade-offs list both gains AND sacrifices (operational complexity, debugging difficulty, eventual consistency). Event schema explicitly identified as rigidity point. Channel extensibility identified as flexibility point. Implementation guidance is highly concrete with phased plan, specific file paths, technology recommendations (SQS/Cloud Tasks/BullMQ), and pitfalls. Small-team constraint factored throughout (rejected Kafka, rejected separate workers per channel, rejected separate microservice). Risks section includes queue infrastructure concerns (poison messages, observability gaps, outbox reliability).

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough trade-offs section with three separate decisions analyzed, each with gains, sacrifices, and alternatives considered
Correctly rejected Kafka/RabbitMQ and CQRS/event-sourcing as over-engineering for a 3-person team
Identified the transactional outbox pattern to solve dual-write problem — shows deep architectural understanding
Implementation guidance is immediately actionable: phased weekly plan, specific file paths, concrete technology options with decision criteria
Event schema identified as rigidity point with mitigation (schema versioning from day one)
Idempotent consumer pattern included to handle at-least-once delivery semantics — a detail many architects miss
Pitfalls section directly addresses common mistakes Shaq might make, preventing rework
Escalations array is empty — could have flagged the unknown cloud provider as needing stakeholder input before implementation begins

agent_output_excerpt:
{
  "executive_summary": "For a small courier team (3 engineers) needing reliable, extensible notifications with relaxed latency requirements, Option B (event-driven with a lightweight queue) is the recommended architecture — but scoped to a managed queue service (e.g., SQS, Cloud Tasks, or BullMQ on Redis) rather than full-blown Kafka/RabbitMQ infrastructure.
